messages.content:
can you give me this inform of SWAY :Sarah is a 12-year-old girl was referred by the rheumatologist to Paediatric Physiotherapy Outpatients to help manage her Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis. Sarah’s symptoms began when she was 10- years old and included pain, stiffness, and swelling in her bilateral knees (Rt>Lt) and bilateral ankles. Her parents were concerned and took her to see her pediatrician, who referred her to a rheumatologist. After a series of tests, Sarah was diagnosed with Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA). Sarah’s rheumatologist prescribed medication to help manage her symptoms. During her assessment with physiotherapist, Sarah reported that her symptoms were affecting her ability to participate in daily activities, such as playing with friends and participating in activities such as running, jumping, and climbing. Her parents also reported that she was becoming more sedentary. Sarah was highly dependent on her parents for activities of daily living. She also had difficulty with mobility, taking stairs and required the use of a wheelchair for longer distances. Also, she has lower limb muscle wasting and she complained of pain during certain movements.
In addition, concerns were also raised by her doctor about her bone density due to her lack of physical activity since her JIA diagnosis. A DEXA scan was ordered, which showed that Sarah had low bone density, indicating a risk for osteoporosis.

Sarah is a 12-year-old girl who has been diagnosed with Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA). Her symptoms include pain, stiffness, and swelling in her bilateral knees (Rt>Lt) and bilateral ankles. Her rheumatologist prescribed medication to help manage her symptoms. However, during her assessment with the physiotherapist, Sarah reported that her symptoms were affecting her ability to participate in daily activities, such as playing with friends and participating in activities such as running, jumping, and climbing. Her parents also reported that she was becoming more sedentary and had difficulty with mobility, taking stairs, and required the use of a wheelchair for longer distances. Additionally, Sarah has lower limb muscle wasting and complains of pain during certain movements. 

Furthermore, Sarah's doctor is concerned about her bone density due to her lack of physical activity since her JIA diagnosis. A DEXA scan was ordered, which showed that Sarah had low bone density and indicating a risk for osteoporosis. To manage her symptoms and improve her overall physical function, Sarah was referred for paediatric physiotherapy outpatients services. The aim of physiotherapy is to improve Sarah's strength, range of motion, and function to enable her to perform daily activities and participate in physical activities. The physiotherapist will also provide education on physical activity, exercises, and joint protection techniques to help manage her arthritis and reduce the risk of osteoporosis.

No one understands what it's like to be a young person with arthritis more than you do. At times, we know that it can be nerve-wracking, confusing, or even a bit lonely.

But we also know that it's possible to live well with arthritis - and that having the right advice and support can make a massive difference.

So, here is your go-to place to learn about living with arthritis as a young person

. Our goal is to provide you with information, resources, and support that will help you manage your condition, understand your options, and connect with others who understand what you're going through. 

You'll find helpful articles and guides on topics like managing arthritis pain, staying active, communicating with your healthcare team, and dealing with the emotional impact of arthritis. We'll also share stories, tips, and advice from other young people who have been diagnosed with arthritis, so you can see how they have overcome the challenges it brings.

In addition, we offer various support groups where you can connect with other young people who have arthritis - share experiences and get advice directly from people who have been there.

Remember, you are not alone. There are many young people facing arthritis, just like you. With the right tools, knowledge, and support, you can live a full and active life, even with arthritis. So, take a look around and make the most of the resources we have to offer!

No one understands what it’s like to be a young person with arthritis more than you do. At times, we know that it can be nerve-wracking, confusing, or even a bit lonely.

But we also know that it’s possible to live well with arthritis - and that having the right advice and support can make a massive difference.

So, here is your go-to place to learn about living with arthritis as a young person Paraphrase

We understand that as a young person with arthritis, you might feel overwhelmed, confused or isolated. However, with the right information and support, you can still enjoy a good quality of life despite the condition. This platform is designed specifically to provide you with the resources you need to manage your arthritis. You will find helpful articles, guides, and support groups to help you understand and deal with the condition's challenges. Ultimately, we aim to create a community where you can connect with other young people with arthritis and share experiences and advice.
How to explain osteoporosis for a child in a kind way ?

Osteoporosis is a condition that can affect bones and make them weak and fragile. It's like having a bone that's easily breakable, kind of like a pencil that can snap very easily. It can happen if someone doesn't eat enough healthy foods, doesn't exercise enough, or has certain illnesses. It's most common in older people, but sometimes it can happen to younger people too. But don't worry, there are things that can be done to make bones stronger, like eating healthy foods with calcium and vitamin D, and doing exercise to keep bones strong. It's important to take care of our bodies so we can stay healthy and strong!
Emotional distress. Living with JIA for 2 years is not easy and sometime you feel depressed or low mood, but it is ok to feel that sometime but we need to keep positive thinking! Is thera any typos?

There is only one typo - "thera" should be "there". The corrected sentence is: "Living with JIA for 2 years is not easy and sometimes you feel depressed or low mood, but it is okay to feel that sometimes. But we need to keep positive thinking!"

Living with JIA for 2 years is not easy and sometimes you feel depressed or low mood, but it is okay to feel that sometimes. But we need to keep positive thinking! -See this Video  to know that this normally happens but we need to deal with that positively. Is there any typos?
There are no typos in this sentence.

Low physical activity. I know that because of pain and fear of making more damage to your joint, you stopped doing any physical activity. That cause your muscle to become weaker and make it even more  harder to move to you. Instead what you need to do is to keep active and enjoy think that you liked to do. Paraphrase?

I understand that due to pain and fear of joint damage, physical activity may have been reduced. This could have caused muscle weakness and further difficulties with movement. However, it is important to stay active and enjoy activities that you like doing, as this will help keep the body moving and increase strength.
